pageTitle.value: Paul Nicklen - Telling our Planet’s Stories - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: Canadian photographer and filmmaker Paul Nicklen has dedicated his life to protecting the polar regions and marine life around the world. Now, with the support of Rolex’s Perpetual Planet Initiative, he will continue to use his stunning photography to raise awareness of the threats faced by life within those ecosystems while inspiring action to protect them.

WaPageTitle.value: Paul Nicklen - Telling our Planet’s Stories - Perpetual Planet

metadescription.value: Photojournalist Paul Nicklen uses visual storytelling to raise awareness of the threats faced by the planet's polar and marine life. More on rolex.org.

text.value: Canadian photographer and filmmaker Paul Nicklen has dedicated his life to protecting the polar regions and marine life around the world. Now, with the support of Rolex’s Perpetual Planet Initiative, he will continue to use his stunning photography to raise awareness of the threats faced by life within those ecosystems while inspiring action to protect them.

Paul Nicklen’s love affair with the polar world started at four years old, when his family moved to Baffin Island in northern Canada. They were one of only three non-Inuit families within a small community, and with no television, telephone or radio for distraction, Nicklen spent most hours out on the sea ice, soaking up the Inuit way of life, observing animals such as seals, polar bears and narwhals, and falling in love with the Arctic environment. That cold, forbidding landscape became his place of comfort.
Growing up in this environment, I could see the ecosystem. I could see how it all worked together. And it deeply impacted me.

The impact of his childhood home was so profound that today Nicklen is one of the world’s leading photojournalists, telling critical stories about the ocean and the precarious state of the polar regions.

He initially worked as a wildlife biologist, but feeling he could have a greater impact, he quit his job to focus on visual storytelling. Instead of spreadsheets, he wanted to portray science and observations through vivid imagery.

To sharpen his photography and storytelling skills, Nicklen did it all: from heading to the Arctic on a three-month solo expedition with an inflatable canoe, some 250 kg of gear and only wolves and bears for company, to getting a job as a tundra buggy driver to support his passion for photography. He also found generous mentors in National Geographic photographers Flip Nicklin and Joel Sartore, who taught him how to tell impactful stories through his visuals. Eventually, Nicklen himself became an assignment photographer for National Geographic.

Now he frequently works alongside scientists, fellow conservationists and oceanographers, translating what they see in far-flung places into visual stories that can move people, no matter where they live. His passion lies not just in taking stunning pictures of landscapes and animals, but in getting urgent stories out; stories that educate the world about the planet’s spectacular polar and marine life, and show the public how threats like climate change and habitat destruction are accelerating biodiversity loss.

Capturing impactful images, though, often takes incredible patience and lots of risks in challenging environments. Nicklen, on assignments for National Geographic, has run out of air on a dive, fallen through thin sea ice and dislocated his shoulder, been in near-fatal plane crashes, suffered multiple cases of frostbite and been charged by animals like grizzly bears and massive elephant seals. Through all these experiences, he has taken some of the most influential photographs of wildlife: from a series on the elusive narwhals of the North Atlantic, to the spirit bears in the giant old-growth cedar forests of British Columbia and leopard seals in Antarctica, including the viral story of how a female leopard seal tried to nurture him by bringing him penguins to eat.

In 2014, along with his partner, renowned Mexican conservation photographer Cristina Mittermeier, and photographer and filmmaker Andy Mann, Nicklen co-founded the non-profit SeaLegacy. The organization’s goal is to support Nicklen, Mittermeier and other photographers to embark on expeditions that capture inspiring and impactful stories of ocean conservation. At the nexus of climate action and sustainable solutions, SeaLegacy creates strategies and content that move audiences into action.

For his sensitive and evocative visual stories, Nicklen has received more than 30 prestigious awards, including the BBC Wildlife Photographer of the Year and six World Press Photo Awards. In 2019, he also became one of the youngest inductees into the International Photography Hall of Fame and Museum.

His efforts towards showcasing the interconnectedness of life in the ocean and how human actions and climate change are derailing it – especially in polar regions – have also earned him widespread recognition. This includes the BioGems Visionary Award by the Natural Resources Defense Council, an Honorary Doctorate of Science from the University of Victoria, British Columbia, induction into the Order of Canada and an Honorary Doctorate of Arts from Simon Fraser University.

With in excess of seven million followers, he is also one of Instagram’s most popular nature photographers. Nicklen uses his social media as a means to educate and urge action. He shares personal stories behind photographs that reveal the struggles of people and animals whose lives are dependent on a healthy ocean.
With every image that inspires others, every successful expedition, conservation win, every recovering species, or ecosystem, I find my heart fill with hope that it is not too late.

In his wildly popular 2011 TED Talk, viewed more than 2.5 million times, he employed striking images to show how climate change is causing polar regions to lose ice and what that means for those fragile ecosystems.

Nicklen has witnessed species disappear, habitats destroyed and people forced to leave their homes as the world warms up. He perceives his visual stories as calls to action, building widespread support for the protection of the Arctic and other regions around the world, the ocean and their wildlife. The Perpetual Planet Initiative and the Canadian photographer are joined in this mission to conserve the future of the planet.

pageCaption.value: Cristina Mittermeier is a pioneer of conservation photography. Now, with the support of Rolex’s Perpetual Planet Initiative, she is continuing to take photographs that spark conversations around the ocean and inspire action to protect it.

metadescription.value: One of today’s most influential conservation photographers, Cristina Mittermeier uses visual storytelling to advocate for the ocean. More on rolex.org.
